food_subgroup: Crustaceans — freshwater prawn, whole, grilled or baked with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for giant freshwater prawn (Macrobrachium rosenbergii) as a whole grilled/baked dish. FOODON_03411433 (shrimp food product) is the closest parent crustacean category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN (Crustaceans) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for giant freshwater prawn as a prepared whole dish in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144001 (Prawn) is the closest parent substance code. No specific code for giant scampi prawn as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: sauce (Thai-style, may include fish sauce, oyster sauce, or butter-based sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole giant scampi prawn (approximately 200 g edible portion including shell-on weight) served grilled or baked with a Thai-style sauce, as typically offered at a seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15152); adapted for giant freshwater prawn (Macrobrachium rosenbergii) with sauce contribution estimated from USDA Foundation Foods butter and garlic sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for giant scampi prawn as a whole grilled/baked dish with sauce. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) as the closest approved crustacean analogue, with modest upward adjustment to fat and sodium to account for butter/oil and sauce contribution typical of restaurant preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; peer-reviewed analogous estimate for cooked crustaceans/shrimp
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for giant scampi prawn as a whole grilled dish. Cooked shrimp and crustaceans are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with other cooked crustaceans and seafood items in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables, which consistently show GI values below 10 for high-protein, near-zero-carbohydrate seafood. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 3.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.15, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of this dish may contribute approximately 840 mg sodium (based on 200 g serving at 420 mg/100 g), which represents a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Giant scampi prawn is a very low-carbohydrate, high-protein food with a glycemic index estimated at 5 (low), making it a suitable protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sauce may contain added sugars or starch thickeners, so patients should confirm sauce ingredients and prefer grilled preparations with minimal sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and saturated fat from butter-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Giant freshwater prawn contains approximately 152 mg cholesterol per 100 g and the butter-based sauce adds saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request oil-reduced preparation, and balance this dish within a diet low in total saturated fat and dietary cholesterol as per AHA guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Giant Scampi Prawn (whole, grilled or baked with sauce) is a premium Thai-style seafood dish featuring Macrobrachium rosenbergii, the giant freshwater prawn, one of the largest freshwater crustaceans cultivated in Southeast Asia. The prawn is typically served whole — head, shell, and roe intact — and is grilled or baked at high heat, then finished with a rich Thai-style sauce that may include butter, garlic, chilli, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, producing the characteristic 'oozing' appearance from the head fat and sauce. The flesh is firm, sweet, and succulent with a mild briny flavour, while the head contains rich, creamy tomalley. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein (approximately 20.5 g per 100 g), low-carbohydrate food with moderate fat content largely attributable to the sauce. Sodium is elevated due to seasoning and sauce, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), making it appropriate for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Cholesterol content is notable at approximately 152 mg per 100 g. The dish is popular in Thai seafood restaurants in Singapore, particularly at establishments specialising in live seafood, and commands a premium price point. It is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and omega-3 fatty acids from the crustacean flesh.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a whole giant prawn?
Can diabetics eat giant scampi prawn?
Is giant freshwater prawn high in cholesterol?
Is scampi prawn high in sodium?
Thai giant prawn calories per piece

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for giant scampi prawn as a whole grilled/baked dish with Thai-style sauce. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) with estimated sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked crustaceans. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and restaurant-specific sauce variability.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) as the closest approved crustacean analogue; fat and sodium have been adjusted upward to account for typical butter/oil and sauce contribution in restaurant preparation. 2. The input food name 'Oozing Giant Scampi Prawn (Per Pc)' is a menu-style name from field survey data; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Giant Scampi Prawn (Whole, Grilled/Baked with Sauce)'. 3. Serving size is estimated at 200 g (whole prawn with shell, head, and sauce) based on typical restaurant portion for a single large Macrobrachium rosenbergii specimen. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in cooked crustacean flesh per USDA data; dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as crustacean flesh contains no dietary fibre per USDA SR Legacy. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy shrimp entry used as the source analogue. 6. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with cooked crustaceans in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for giant scampi prawn exists in the approved GI literature. 7. Halal status is marked true for the prawn itself; sauce ingredients (fish sauce, oyster sauce) are generally halal but may vary by restaurant. 8. Price of SGD 51.80 per piece from field survey data (Shrimp Prawn Seafood — Boat Quay) is preserved as contextual metadata from the input record.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from menu-style 'Oozing Giant Scampi Prawn (Per Pc)' to canonical 'Giant Scampi Prawn (Whole, Grilled/Baked with Sauce)'.
Added Thai-script local name and romanized form as required by G6 for Thai cuisine.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for LLM retrieval coverage.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp as closest approved analogue with documented adaptation rationale.
Set GI to 5 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked crustaceans, with full documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) and all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g) with justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Used only approved sources (USDA FoodData Central, University of Sydney GI Database).
Confirmed all three ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (closest_parent).
